draft.introduction: Mumonkan Case 7: “Joshu’s Wash Your Bowl.” A monk asks for instruction. Joshu asks if he has eaten his rice gruel. Yes. “Then wash your bowl.” The monk was enlightened.

translation.text: A monk said to Joshu: “I have just entered this monastery. I beg you, Master, please give me instruction.” Joshu said: “Have you eaten your rice gruel?” The monk said: “Yes, I have.” Joshu said: “Then go and wash your bowl.” At that the monk was enlightened.
translation.original_text: 趙州因僧問、某甲新到此院、乞師指示

translator_notes.note: The koan’s genius is its refusal to be special. The monk seeks esoteric teaching; Joshu points to the most mundane act possible. Enlightenment is not a state beyond washing bowls but the full presence within washing bowls. This is the Zen teaching of “nothing special.”
